sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Foreign Body Airway Obstruction (FBAO), also known as foreign body aspiration, is a medical emergency that occurs when a foreign object becomes lodged in the airway, blocking the flow of air. FBAO is a serious medical emergency that requires prompt treatment. With a complete foreign body airway obstruction, unconsciousness can occur within 3 to 6 minutes, with death likely within 5 to 10 minutes if the obstruction isn't cleared.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
Mild (Partial Obstruction) Severe (Complete) Obstruction
- Effective Cough
- Audible breathing/air movement
- Ability to speak or vocalise
- Wheeze, stridor
- Dysphagia, drooling
- Anxiety, agitation, gasping, clutching at throat
- Paradoxical chest / abdominal movement
- Ineffective or absent cough
- Inability to speak or vocalise
- Silent Chest
- Cyanosis
- Clutching at throat
- Rapid deterioration in consciousness
- Apnoea or cardiac arrest if unresolved
- Difficulty when moving air during IPPV
- History of event that can cause FBAO, e.g., eating, or playing with smaller objects.

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- The severity of obstruction is judged primarily by cough effectiveness and ability to move air
- Effective cough suggests partial obstruction and should usually be managed by encouraging cough and close observation
- Ineffective cough, inability to speak, silent chest, or rapidly worsening distress suggests severe obstruction
- Manage severe FBAO according to the current ANZCOR choking algorithm and local protocol
- Do not perform blind finger sweeps
- Do not leave the patient unattended
- Evidence for suction-based anti-choking devices is limited and they should never delay standard recommended manoeuvres

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey

**Effective cough:**
  - Place the patient in a position of comfort
  - Encourage coughing
  - Reassure continuously and watch closely for deterioration

**Severe obstruction:**
  - If conscious, give 5 back blows and check after each blow
  - If still obstructed, give 5 chest thrusts
  - Continue alternating back blows and chest thrusts while the patient remains conscious and the obstruction persists
  - If the patient becomes unconscious, commence CPR and inspect the mouth only when visible obstruction can be safely removed
  - Prepare for rapid deterioration and cardiac arrest
  - Escalate early for advanced airway support

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider laryngoscopy or Magill forceps removal only if the foreign body is directly visualised and within scope or local protocol
- Consider front-of-neck airway options only within local scope, equipment, and protocol for a cannot-oxygenate situation

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content: - Commonly, a foreign body may be lodged at the top of the oesophagus mimicking an airway obstruction
